speaker: interviewer
text: And do you like your name pronounced 'Rubinstein' or 'Rubinsteen'?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-U-B-I- N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you pronounce that -stine or -steen?

speaker: interviewee
text: -steen.

speaker: interviewer
text: -steen. OK. That's how you pronounce it. I just wanted to make sure. The survivor we're interviewing is Donna Rubinstein. The date, February 21st, 1997. The interview is being conducted in Miami, Florida in the United States. And the interviewer is Joan Baker.

words.text: I just wanted to make sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Joan Baker. I'm interviewing survivor Donna Rubinstein. The date is February 21st, 1997. The interview is being conducted in Miami, Florida in the United States, and it will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give us your name please and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm Donna Rubinstein-- D-O-N-N-A R-U-B-I-N-S-T-E-I-N. I was born on January 30th, in 1923 in Krasnostav.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us where Krasnostav is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krasnostav was on the border from Eastern Ukraine and Poland. It's a small village, approximately 800 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of those 800 people, how many were Jewish? What was the ratio of Jewish to Christian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish people lived in one area. And it could be maybe about 500, I would say. And the Ukraine people lives in that area, not too far. You know, walking distance at the village. And they were about 300, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jews and Christians get along in Krasnostav?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. They got along very well. Trade in merchandise, helping each other with the-- especially with cooperation of the Jewish holidays. The Christians came and helped them. And it was because existence was very good. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like? What did your family do and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born to a very Orthodox religious, Orthodox Jewish family. And I went to public school in the Russian public school, where I taught that religion is an option for the people. And I came home and they taught me the different things-- that religion is important. And I didn't know whom I should believe. I still my [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went off to school, could you describe what the community did for religious holidays and what your family did-- what you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember we didn't have too much. We lived a very poor life. But when it comes to holiday, we get together whatever we can get. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We prepared the food. We cleaned the house, and everything was clockwise. And of course, they asked me to help them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And on other holidays, which was forbidden from the government-- the government didn't allowed this. I remember being in school as a small girl, about 8 or 10 years old. I-- I took part in celebrations. I used to make like a place against the religion, that the religion is not worth it. Nothing. And they ask me I should help them out, and I had to do this. And Saturdays, I had to go to school, which the family did not allow me to do this. And it wasn't very pleasant for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a real conflict there between a very religious family and the state?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the school and the state, correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about your family, about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only one-- the only one daughter of my parents. And they lived very poorly. But I remember winter was very cold. We didn't have enough fuel or wood to warm up the house. And I-- but most of the time I was in my grandmother's house. They had a bigger house, and it was warmer there, and I run to the grandmother. And also she gave me something to eat, which I didn't have too much in my home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't have sisters or brothers, but I have lots of uncles and aunts, and uncles, and nephews, nieces. And they kept me going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were-- your cousins were like your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of an extended family lived-- did everyone live in this village, or were they in neighboring villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I know they lived in that village. Of course, neighboring villages were where my grandmother from my father's side lives, about 40 kilometers from my village. That was a different village, Annopol. And in summers when the school-- when I was out from school, I went to there. To the grandmother from my father's side. There I made a set of different friends, and I liked it there very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had both sets of grandparents then living very close to you or with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your father? What do you remember of your father and what he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my father was a very religious man-- very religious. Twice a day, he went to the synagogue-- in the morning and in the evening. He couldn't provide too much for a living. Before this Soviets took over the government, he had a small store, which was a dowry from my grandfather. Then they come-- the Soviets, they took it away. And he couldn't sell anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used to be-- he was a peddler, peddling merchandise to the Christians, to the people in the village. And he made a very poor living by that. And the house was very cold in the winter. We didn't have enough wood to heat the house. And like I said, I spent most of the days by my grandmother. So you could keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warm, and then I had the room to talk with my uncles and aunts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your father get the merchandise that he would peddle?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was illegal. They got it from some-- maybe his brother from the United States. They did send him once, twice, or three times a year some packages from the United States. And when he got them-- let's see. Some shoes, or some kerchiefs, and things like that. And he sold it. He peddled, and he sold it to the farmers in the village. He made a very poor living by that. He traded.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had had family then who emigrated to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had family who emigrated in 1921. One brother, the oldest brother, came here in 19-- after the war. In 1915, after the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two brothers, they send us packages about three times, approximately. Three times a year. And that kept us alive. It helped a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one thing when I was a small girl. They came to the house to search the house, because in the post office somebody told them that we received a few American dollars, maybe $10. So they came to look all over the house. They did find the money hidden in a little bottle. And they took my father away for a few weeks in the KGB, because we couldn't trade with American-- with a capitalistic country. So it was wrong for the government. And also for that reason, the night when I was older-- a few years older-- and I wanted to be a Pioneer, they didn't accept me. Or Komsomols-- they didn't accept me to that the organization because my father, my parents, had the correspondence with the United States, with a capitalistic country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the one hand, it helped you survive. But on the other hand, it caused problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: It did cause problems, yes. It did cause problems. But the only thing about the Soviets, what say, they gave me after I finished at seven years of school in my native place, in my native village. I could go on to study in a bigger city-- where they gave me a  stipend and a dormitory I shared with other girls. For my parents times, this wasn't allowed. They didn't do this. But for the Soviets, they did. So it was a bad in one way, and it was good in another. That's what it is. For young people, it wasn't bad. But for the older people, it was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your parents' education under the old system?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were minimum. And my mother very minimum education. My father, he has a Jewish education. Not too much in the Russian language. They weren't educated in the Russian language.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother? What do you remember of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember-- very slight person, very working very difficult. It was difficult for her to get the things into the house to make a meal for the holidays. She struggled very hard-- very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had life been like in this village before your time? If your uncles emigrated, had things always been pretty much the same or had there been problems before that caused them to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uncles-- my two uncle emigrated because they didn't want to go to the army. And they-- of course, it was difficult to get jobs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nothing what to do in the village-- it was very few jobs where you can get. And of course, they were looking for opportunities and they sent away. They went to the United States. But I suppose they weren't as religious as my father, because my father wouldn't have gone here. Because the going away from my native land, from Ukraine, to the United States means to give up religion in many ways. And he refused. He wouldn't do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two brothers went after the war. And he remained, my father remained with his mother and a younger brother. The youngest baby brother was still there in Russia, in Ukraine. So religion was really the heart of the family and the village it seems.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The shtetl, yes. How many synagogues were in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was two synagogues. But I remember it was the Soviets, they come and they say and they went and they destroyed. They tore out all the religious books. They throw outside. They said, we don't have no use. They are no use for us. And what I remember, my grandmother-- I put it in the book even. The grandmother went and she brought in a few religious books, and she kept them in one of the homes covered with her nice tablecloth. And the same was with the church. They didn't allow churches also. So the young Komsomols they climbed up, and they tore the crosses away. This was the government against religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that oppression against religion-- do you think in a way that contributed to the fairly good relations between the Christians and Jews in the shtetl because both were denied that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both were denied. And then they-- somehow they trade with each other. They mingled. I remember on the cold and winter days they used to come to the shtetl. And they used to stop by our-- my grandmother's house. And they were having tea with cookies, whatever the grandmother served them. It was-- there was very good coexistence between the gentile people and the Jewish people-- only till the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But would you say that the Christian community was also fairly religious as the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are very-- I used to see them Sunday in early in the morning going to church. And also the holidays. They used observe their holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: But all of this changed with the war then, those relations?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. All of this changed when the Communist government took over. As they took over, they-- after the revolution. In the '20s, everything changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your education? Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years of school I went to a Jewish school. Jewish language was the main subject. We all said once a week or twice week Ukrainian language. And the same as in the schools here-- arithmetic, geography, and history and everything were taught there by the Russians. Then after seven years, I went to a bigger city, [UKRAINIAN]. And I wanted to be a school teacher. So the government--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then when you left home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 16 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they helped me a lot with education. They provided books for me for free, and also  I had the dormitories to share with the other girls a room. We had-- we didn't have private baths, so we shared it with others. And the coexistence wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how far away from this-- from your home was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was over 100 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a long way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't have a train there. I mean, we went first of all-- I didn't go by plane. This was not existent. So with the train, it took-- first of all, we have to take a horse and buggy to come to the train station, which was a 10 kilometer, a 15 kilometer. And from there board a train. And it took us a whole night to arrive to the destination, to the city where I was supposed to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were far from home at that age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right. I didn't have a telephone. I couldn't commute to that, to call them up. So we had to just write a letter once in awhile. And of course, I didn't have too much to eat. I had-- they gave us twice a day something. In the morning, a little breakfast. And then we had in the afternoon a meal. The main  meal was in the afternoon-- dinner-- in a restaurant  for all the students. We used to have coupons. So they gave us the coupons which provided the food. And the girls were all right. We didn't have enough books though, so we had to share. Three girls were supposed to share one book. So that's what the school years went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the number of Jewish students at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a matter of fact, it was a majority of Jewish students.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a lot of Jewish students. And-- I didn't-- at that time, I didn't see the difference between Jewish students and non-Jewish students. We were all students, and that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But religion was strongly discouraged?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. With religion they-- when I was-- when I was in higher school, in teachers college, they didn't taught us religion. It wasn't a subject. We didn't even-- we didn't think that religion exist at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with the message that you were getting at school and your family who was deeply religious? And you were very young then. How do you remember dealing with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember in school was very difficult, because I had to go in my shtetl. I had to go to school on Saturdays. My grandfather didn't want me to go to school, so I lied to him and I said, I'm only reading there. I'm not using the pen. I'm not writing. And he wasn't pleased with that. Always he checked my fingers if there was ink on the fingers, because our pens were [NON-ENGLISH]. [LAUGHS] And the-- I remember we made that play once before one of the holidays. And we showed that the people, that the religion is not good for the-- in general for the population. And like we said, it was in Jewish we said it. It was a little poem like that. That the-- [NON-ENGLISH] It was, you know, it can't. To show them that the-- we shouldn't believe in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you taught to believe in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taught to believe in the government. Believe in Stalin that he is our father, and we should believe in him. And he puts us, he leads us in the right way. That's what I was taught in school. Well, the contrast, the opposite. What I was when I came home, I heard a different language. So I didn't know. I was torn between the school and the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you want to do when you were away at school? What were your dreams at that time? You wanted to be a teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to be a teacher. And I was prepared to be a teacher until the war started in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the group you said before? You had wanted to join a group. And you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to join a group like Komsomol. But they didn't accept me for the simple reason, what my parents have brought us in the United States. And my parents are religious. They are not the proper people, and I've been a daughter from the enemy of the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they didn't accept me. And I was very much crying. I was very much upset by that incident. Sometimes I wished I was-- they would kill me rather than to leave me without being in Komsomol.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would it have meant to have gotten into that group? Can you describe that group? What it did?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the Komsomol?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Komsomol was an organization. It's like here they-- first of all, it was before Komsomol was Pioneers. The Pioneers was like here the Girls Scouts and Boy Scouts. Komsomol is already a higher step, a step higher. And they-- to be decent citizens, to obey the rules from the Communist rules, and to love the country, and to love the leader of the country, Joseph Stalin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a youth political group. It was very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. It was a youth-- it was a political group. That's right. And unfortunately, I couldn't join.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after you found out? You went for this interview, and you found out you couldn't join.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very upset. I was to a certain degree upset. That I was even thinking about committing suicide. At a certain point, I was upset by that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was not in the Komsomol. And they-- of course, I didn't have the-- I wanted to say if it's a meeting or something-- I couldn't join them. I was an outcast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were an outcast among the other students at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was there left for you to do then? What did you do in terms of a job, or did you leave school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. No, I continued school. I didn't leave school. But they looked all the way down at me for that simple reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how much longer were you in school to finish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must've been about two years. Close to two years, something like that. And then when I went away, they-- I mean, when I was in teacher's college, I tried my best to have good grades. I shouldn't be less important than the other girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your best subjects? What were the things you were best at?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked the geography. I liked history. I didn't like too much chemistry or the science subject I didn't like too much. Literature I liked. Where you studied a lot of Literature. I studied the history of the-- the old history from Russian history from before. And the geography I liked very much. I always looked on the map to find where my uncle lived in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about languages? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Language we-- our main language was Ukrainian. We had twice a week Russian language. It's a little different than Ukrainian. And then later, in the later years, we had the German. Once a week, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that helped you later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, of course. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What-- when did you graduate? Did you get a diploma of any kind from this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- Yes. I got the diploma in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after I was prepared to be a schoolteacher. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the war caught me in the process of being-- they sent me to a village. I should teach children. And the war caught me up at that. That's it. This was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- so you actually started teaching.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I did start.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did have a teaching job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the wages were-- the salary what very, very poorly for teachers. It was 240 rubles, which is very little money, you know. We could hardly make a living. But it was a prestigious job. It wasn't like that, you know. You look up to a teacher in my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had you wanted to teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to teach in a big city. But when I went there to the board of education, they didn't grant me the jobs. They said that we need teachers for the village children, too. You cannot be-- not everybody could be in a bigger city. And then what I wasn't in Komsomol. If I would have been in Komsomol, maybe they would have sent me to a bigger city. I wanted to teach in Kiev, in a big city. But I-- but I was refused that job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just by way of your connections with your uncles, that really caused you that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was bad. Yes. This was very, very bad for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first sense of the war? When did the war first make an impact on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war started on the 22nd of June, 1941. I was on vacation. It was summer. I was in the house with my parents. When the war started, we didn't have radios. We didn't have televisions. We didn't have no way of knowing what's going on. But they-- I-- I didn't know what to do at that time. I could've left my parents and to go to run away to a bigger city like other people, but I didn't want to leave my parents. I didn't want to leave them alone, and I remained with them till the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop right here. OK.
